1: The wise listen before they speak. Your Wisdom shapes how long you have to answer the dungeon's questions — every point of it above the common measure buys you another breath of time.
Your body burns through its reserves with every step. When the hunger gnaws deep enough, it begins to take something more than comfort.
Scrolls crumble after a single reading, their knowledge spent. But a spellbook is a patient teacher — it will share its secret as many times as you ask.

Fire changes everything it touches. A wise traveler learns to cook early — the difference between raw flesh and a proper meal can mean the difference between dragging yourself forward and marching in strength.
The ? key is the beginning of wisdom for those new to the dungeon. Press it and let the dungeon explain itself.
A lockpick is a delicate instrument. Use it clumsily and it will betray you at the worst moment — fragile ones break first.
Not every chest a dungeon holds is left in plain sight. Some rewards wait behind locked lids in forgotten corners.
Treasures rest where they fall. Stand over what you wish to carry and coax it into your hands with G or a comma.
The deeper you descend, the greater the peril — but also the greater the prize. The dungeon rewards the bold who survive long enough to claim it.

Not every wound is made the same way. Blades that slash, points that pierce, and weights that crush each find different purchase on different flesh. A warrior who carries only one kind of weapon is only half a warrior.
Strength is the measure of what you can carry and how long your body endures the burden of moving through the world. Do not neglect it.
A hardy constitution is the simplest armor of all — it is written into your blood and bones before you ever set foot underground.
Some doors in the dungeon open only when you already know where you are going. Maps and memory are their own kind of key.
The dungeon does not announce its traps. A careful eye and a slow step save more lives than a quick sword arm.
Some say the dungeon itself is a kind of program — and all programs have hidden inputs. Not every key on your keyboard does what you’d expect. Try the ones that seem to do nothing.
2: Fire and cold are ancient enemies. A creature born of flame laughs at your torch but shivers before your frost, and what walks in ice will melt before an ember.
Keen eyes see further into the dark and shoot further into it as well. Perception is the gift of the wary.
Confusion is a thief that steals your time at the worst possible moment. Clear your mind before the questions begin.
The mind is a vessel. A keen intellect fills it with more arcane power — and when philosophy or science is the subject of the quiz, the sharp-minded linger a little longer over each answer.
Cooking the flesh of your enemies is not savagery — it is wisdom. What they carried in their bodies, you carry forward. The deeper the beast, the richer the meal.
The staircase landing is a place of quiet power. Those who rest where floors change find their bodies knitting themselves back together without the cost of a potion.
The undead are past the reach of much that wounds the living. Fire and holy power remind them that they are not entirely beyond consequence.
A weapon with reach is worth twice its weight in a narrow corridor. Strike the monster before it can answer in kind.
A wand holds only so much of the world’s power before it goes silent. Do not waste its voice on unworthy targets.
Some accessories are worth more than armor. The right ring on the right finger turns a blade or sheds a cold that might otherwise kill you.
The body’s endurance is not only about surviving wounds. Stamina is the measure of how long you can keep moving — and strength adds to the well you draw from.

Different corners of the dungeon test different kinds of knowledge. Those who arm themselves for armor and history, for accessories and the stories of ancient nations, fare better than those who study only one thing.

Items found in the dark are not always what they appear. Some are blessed beyond their nature, others carry a curse that clings until something holy drives it out. Altars may reveal what your eyes cannot.
Strange altars sometimes appear in the dungeon. Those who approach and kneel before them discover ancient challenges — and ancient rewards.
Polearms and staves can strike enemies beyond arm’s reach. A spearman need not stand toe-to-toe with a beast to draw blood.
The strength of a monster’s body can be drawn into your own if you know how to harvest it. But harvesting is a skill the dungeon tests before it rewards.
Prayer is not merely a comfort for the frightened. Those who speak to the gods at the right place and the right time receive answers more tangible than peace of mind.
A potion does what you need when you swallow it. A spell does what you need whenever you choose to cast it. One is a gift; the other is a discipline.
Elemental resistances are the quiet armor beneath your armor. Accessories that grant them are worth far more than their weight in gold.

Philosophers speak of a reality beneath reality — a hidden terminal that accepts a spoken word. The key to reach it is not listed in any help screen. It sits beside the number 1, quiet and overlooked.
3: Wolf-type ingredients combined with deep cave fungi are said to produce meals of considerable physical strength.
Two healing ingredients combined in a recipe create a restorative meal more powerful than either alone.
Dragon-type ingredients, when carefully cooked, produce meals that greatly increase the body’s resilience to harm.
Ingredients from flying creatures combined with herbs often create meals that sharpen the mind and quicken thought.
Bone-based ingredients combined with cave mushrooms are said to fortify the body’s endurance beyond its natural limits.
Undead-derived ingredients, when properly prepared, can grant the living a temporary taste of the dead’s strange immunities.
The deeper the dungeon, the stronger the recipe ingredients. A humble recipe made with fearsome parts becomes something extraordinary.
Some recipes demand three or more ingredients before they yield their full power. Experiment freely — the dungeon’s kitchen rewards the bold.
Aquatic creature ingredients combined with underground plants can create meals that clear the fog of confusion from a troubled mind.
Fire-type and cold-type ingredients balanced together in the same pot create a meal said to improve all qualities of the body and mind equally.
A fire-bringer in a starlit robe, sashed in gold, is said to walk as a hidden character. His endurance is remarkable and his legacy burns eternal.
One hidden character owns almost nothing: bald, simply robed, and unfazed by hardship. His wisdom is so great that questions seem to slow down around him.
A cunning wanderer in a hood, said to have survived ten years of misadventure at sea, begins the dungeon with exceptional intellect and a hunter’s eye for danger.
A philosopher-mathematician in forest green, with a love of numbers and harmonics, is said to walk the dungeon as a hidden character with a brilliantly sharp intellect.
There are rare creatures that appear in the dungeon only once, if at all — monsters that do not follow the ordinary rules of descent. Treat their appearance as both a warning and an opportunity.
Diogenes carried nothing but a lantern and his convictions. Those who willingly walk in darkness sometimes find wisdom in what they surrendered.
A spider of unusual size and cunning lurks in the early depths. Her web holds even armored warriors in place — but webs have a well-known enemy.
Something half-woman and half-serpent has been reported in the depths. She drains the warmth from those she embraces, leaving them cold long after she is gone.
A bronze giant walks slowly through certain levels, shrugging off every arrow and blade. Travelers who survived noticed a curious imperfection somewhere in its construction.
A serpent-woman who mothers all monsters poisons with every bite. Those who have faced her and lived wore protection against her particular gift.
Reports of a forest spirit that lures wanderers to their doom — old stories suggest cold iron disturbs such beings. Do not follow the music.
A bat-god from the underworld has been seen blinding adventurers with its wings. Conventional arms seem to pass through it like smoke.
A lone sphinx in the depths poses riddles to those bold enough to listen. The wise know what subject she favors, and come prepared.
Ancient Greek smiths spoke of an unfinished hammer buried deep. Where thunder echoes, dwarven work awaits.
Alchemists sought the power to turn lead to gold. Their crucibles still burn in the shallower depths — seek one out and you may find what they left behind.
A Celtic cauldron of transformation is said to demand meals as tribute before it yields its gifts. Cook well before you seek it.
Pandora’s box is said to lie somewhere in the depths. The old stories say opening it was a mistake — but old stories are not always right.
The Wandering Jew walked forever without rest. Those who have walked as far underground are said to perceive things others cannot.
Cerberus guarded the underworld’s gate and knew every soul that passed through it. Those who pass through enough doors earn his grudging respect.
The Sphinx was defeated not by strength but by answering clearly while blind to everything else.
Musashi’s greatest duels were won with restraint. One precise strike, not a storm of blows, showed his mastery.
Rasputin survived poison, bullet, and blade before finally falling. Those who dance with death often enough grow harder to kill.
Cassandra’s prophecies were true even when her methods seemed strange. Even wrong answers can lead to right outcomes, if you press on.
Tantalus was punished by eternal hunger. Those who persist through repeated failure find an iron resolve that cannot be starved out of them.
Orpheus walked among the dead with only his music to protect him. Sometimes the bravest thing is to stand still and let the song carry you.
The Buddha sat in perfect stillness beneath the bodhi tree, unmoved by every threat. Patience in danger is its own form of armor.
Hephaestus returned to his forge obsessively, perfecting the same piece again and again. Repetition is not tedium — it is the smith’s meditation.
Fountains in the dungeon offer more than a drink of water to those willing to answer for it. The thirsty who come with knowledge leave refreshed in more ways than one.
In 1976, a game was released that hid a secret word deep underground. Those who found it could bend the rules of the world. The dungeon remembers old games.
4: The Minotaur haunts the labyrinth at the twentieth depth. He strikes from nowhere and vanishes through the walls — none can corner him in his own maze. But an old myth tells of a thread that once tamed the labyrinth itself.

The Gorgon waits at the fortieth depth. Her gaze freezes all who meet her eyes, and she never stands still. In the old stories, heroes found ways to fight without looking — or to turn her own terrible power against her.

The Grey Sisters share a single eye between three bodies. It has been spotted in the floors before the Gorgon’s domain. Perseus once stole it — and traded it for something far more valuable than sight.

The dragon Fafnir guards the sixtieth depth. His scales are nearly impenetrable — no element, no enchantment seems to help. But in the old tales, Sigurd struck from below, where the scales are softest.
The great wolf Fenrir prowls near the eightieth depth. Survivors describe a creature of terrible and growing endurance — even the gods struggled to bind him.

Those who have fought Fenrir and lived say the same thing: he grew stronger with every passing moment. By the end, his jaws could swallow the sky. Speed alone will not save you — you must find a way to bind the beast.

The dwarves of Svartalfheim forged Gleipnir from six things that do not exist. Adventurers have found strange objects in unusual rooms between the dragon’s lair and the wolf’s den — things that by all rights should not be real.
A forge of dwarven make has been discovered in the deep floors before Fenrir’s hall. The dwarves built wonders from impossible parts — perhaps their forge still remembers the old methods.

Something ancient and terrible waits at the deepest levels. Those who have glimpsed it say conventional weapons are meaningless. Only the devout, those who have earned the right to wield sacred arms, have any hope.
Bosses tend to occupy unusually large, irregular chambers — rooms that feel different from the dungeon’s ordinary architecture, as if the stone itself made way for something significant.
Each boss guards a threshold. Defeating them may not scatter treasure at your feet, but the passage below opens — and sometimes, the real reward is what waits further down.
What you cannot see cannot harm you — or so the legend goes. Blindness is usually a curse, but against certain foes it may be a desperate salvation.
A bronze idol of a bull has been found in the floors before the labyrinth. King Minos offended Poseidon by refusing to sacrifice such a bull. Sacred waters remember old debts.
The mightiest of Greek warriors, near-invulnerable, is hidden among the secret characters — golden armor and crimson plume mark an exceptional warrior whose heel is his only secret.
A Spartan warrior who held a narrow pass against overwhelming odds is a hidden character — bronze helm, scarlet cape, and an iron constitution define his legend.
A great conqueror in golden crown and royal purple cape, who never lost a battle, is hidden among the builds — a formidable all-around warrior who studied both philosophy and war.
The hero who once slew the Minotaur walks as a hidden character — his helmet and noble lineage mark him as a balanced warrior who feels strangely at home in labyrinths.
A winged divine messenger in silver-blue, gifted with unmatched speed and cunning, is a hidden rogue — lighter on his feet than any other character in the dungeon.

Ancient theology speaks of a second death — one the oldest texts say even Death himself cannot escape. Revelation may be the beginning of wisdom for those patient enough to observe, gather, and understand what they face at the very bottom.
A riddle-asking guardian has been encountered around the thirty-fifth depth. It offers a test before combat — those who pass gain something rare; those who fail pay dearly for the privilege.
A son of the forge god roams the mid-depths, breathing fire. He shares his father’s weakness to the element that stands opposite his breath.
The invulnerable lion of ancient myth appears in the dungeon. Blades and arrows bounce off its hide without purchase. Heracles did not use either — and he still walked away.
A Slavic witch with iron teeth heals as she fights, closing her wounds faster than you can open them. The old stories say iron and flame are what she fears most.
A great serpent juvenile has been reported in the deeper levels, its venom legendary. The old tales say only the sky’s own fury could wound such creatures.
A chaos witch in the mid-depths drains magical power from those she touches. The devout and the fiery have fared best against her particular hunger.
An Egyptian servant of death drains life itself and dwells in darkness. The living oppose it with light and faith — those who carry both have the better chance.
The knights of old sought the Grail in dungeons far below. The worthy must know the ways of faith before the cup reveals itself.
Somewhere below the thirtieth level, an Oracle offers visions for tribute. Gold opens prophetic eyes — but the Oracle does not offer change.
A sealed tribunal near the thirtieth depth judges the wise on the great deeds of history. Come prepared, or come not at all.
Jason brought back the Fleece by taming beasts, not slaying them. Knowledge of animals is the key to some of the dungeon’s most stubborn gates.
A well exists somewhere in the depths where wisdom can be purchased, but it demands sacrifice. Some things cannot be bought with gold alone.
Tiresias was blind yet saw more than any sighted prophet. There is power in answering what you cannot see — the dungeon occasionally offers you exactly this challenge.
Penelope wove and unwove her tapestry to delay her suitors. Patience with your equipment, returning to it again and again, rewards the persistent.
The Valkyrie chose who died in battle and valued precision above all. Those who kill from afar honor her philosophy.
Merlin learned his craft by experimenting with the unknown. The unidentified wand in your hand is already teaching you something, whether you realize it or not.
Scheherazade spun tales from books she had not yet finished. Sometimes you must act before you fully understand what you hold.
Crowther and Woods hid a word inside a massive cave. It made no sense to the uninitiated — but it changed everything for those who spoke it aloud in the right place.
Paracelsus, father of toxicology, believed that enduring the poison was the path to immunity. The dungeon agrees with him.
Siegfried bathed in dragon blood and became harder to wound. Those who consume what their enemies inflict may gain unexpected resilience.
Job endured every affliction without losing faith. The dungeon holds many kinds of traps — the faithful survive them all, one way or another.
Asclepius learned the art of healing from serpents. Those who harvest widely from venomous creatures may discover his secret recipe.
The Fisher King’s wound could only be healed by the right prayer at the right moment of desperation. The dungeon has its own wounded kings.
Dionysus saw truth in intoxication. A distorted mind may perceive things a clear one cannot — hallucination is not always the enemy it seems.
Apollo’s arrows never missed. Those who maintain perfect chains of correct answers in battle earn a god’s notice.
Athena’s owl observed all living things with equal patience. Knowledge of many creatures is a form of worship — and worship has its rewards.
Loki wore curses like cloaks and changed them freely. Enduring what others discard as worthless has its own power in the dungeon.
Thor never parted from his hammer and never changed its name. Loyalty to a single weapon forges a bond that raw power alone cannot.
Beowulf tore Grendel’s arm off with his bare hands. Sometimes the mightiest weapon is having none at all.
The Norns wove fate from memory and prophecy. Those who seek lore often enough learn to read the threads before they are cut.
Jormungandr encircled the world, always returning to itself. Some bonds between warrior and weapon deepen through repetition until they cannot be broken.
Shiva dances the dance of illusion without rest. Those who endure hallucination long enough see through the veil to what lies beneath.
Enkidu was born of the wilderness and knew every creature by name. A harvester of many species earns wild wisdom.
Circe transformed her guests through impossible cuisine. Mastering many kinds of cooking is its own form of dangerous magic.
Gawain’s code demanded he fight even when bleeding. Victory won in desperation marks a true knight.
Kali’s dance is patient and eternal — she returns to the same foe again and again without tiring. Persistence is a weapon the dungeon does not always expect.
Persephone mastered the bounty of two worlds. The greatest cooks in the dungeon draw from the widest variety of sources.

Not every encounter in the dungeon is hostile. Some of the souls you meet are simply lost, or hurt, or desperate. What you choose to do when you meet them is between you and whatever judges such things at the end.
5: The Philosopher’s Stone lies at the deepest depth. Retrieve it and ascend — that is the whole of the quest, and the whole of the difficulty.
Prayer at a sacred altar carries more weight than prayer anywhere else. The devout grow wiser through repeated communion, and the gods remember those who remember them.
Abaddon resists nearly everything the dungeon can throw at him. Those who survived speak of faith as the only weapon that drew blood — a blade that was earned, not found.

Theseus survived the labyrinth because Ariadne gave him a thread. In the old stories, it all began with a sacrifice at sacred waters — and ended with a gift that revealed every hidden passage in the maze.

Perseus defeated the Gorgon with a mirror and a blindfold. The Grey Sisters’ Eye was the price he paid for divine aid. Altars remember what is offered to them — and the gods are not always subtle about what they want.

Sigurd slew Fafnir by digging beneath him and striking upward through the soft belly. Before the killing blow, he carried a broken blade — and before the blade was whole, a god had to intervene. Odin’s methods are not always what you’d expect.
Fenrir’s hide turns aside most blows. Enchantment and relentless chains of correct answers are the wolf-binders’ tools — the ribbon was made of impossible things, and so must your preparation be.
The deepest dungeon levels contain provisions that sharpen a warrior’s reflexes to an extraordinary degree. Those who eat well before a boss fight carry the meal’s strength into the battle.

A character who masters both Wisdom and Intelligence can cast powerful spells while enjoying the most generous quiz timers in the dungeon. The philosopher-mage is not a contradiction — it is a tradition.
The divine reward the faithful with permanent wisdom. Three boons are available to those who pray at the right altars with the right knowledge — and the gods do not offer them twice.
Status effects on monsters are not merely inconveniences for them — they are openings. Even a moment of confusion, blindness, or paralysis changes the course of a fight.

The man who catalogued the natural world and tutored a great conqueror is a hidden character — high intellect and wisdom, scholarly beard, blue robe. He begins with a ring and a lantern, as a philosopher should.

A bald, bearded philosopher who famously claimed to know nothing is among the hidden characters. His extraordinary Wisdom provides the most generous quiz timers of any build in the dungeon — he knows how to take his time.

A sage who wrote of caves, shadows, and ideal forms walks as a hidden philosopher character — high intellect, purple robe, and a beard that speaks of long contemplation. He begins with a wand of light and a spellbook.

A dark-robed philosopher who declared that suffering builds strength walks as a hidden character. His build is austere but psychologically formidable, and he begins with the power to make himself stronger.
A legendary archmage in a star-studded dark robe carries a staff and begins already knowing two powerful spells. He is the most magically gifted of all hidden characters — and the most dangerous.
The Green Knight regenerates. Wounds close as fast as you can open them — unless something stops the healing entirely. Not every kind of damage is the same kind of wound.
The great whirlpool beast pulls everything toward its maw and grinds what it catches. Those who survived say it feared the storm more than any blade — the sky’s fury reached even below the waves.

A severed arm of a demon king strikes three times for every one of yours. It has a weakness to purifying force — and a nasty habit of paralyzing those it touches until they can no longer answer at all.
The Wendigo devours stamina itself — you will starve mid-fight if you are not prepared. Old hunters who faced it carried provisions and trusted in fire to drive back what the cold had made.
The Wild Hunt rides through the deepest corridors. Phantom things fear iron and faith — and the captain of the Hunt fears them most of all.
Anansi the trickster confuses and relocates his prey before they can strike back. His webs burn. His illusions shatter under sufficient force — but only if you can find where he has moved you.
A fragment of the World-Serpent carries acid that eats through armor as though it were cloth. Ancient remedies and the sky’s own power are the only answers the old stories offer.
Perseus held his shield as a mirror. Turning an enemy’s power against them is a feat the dungeon rewards more than once — and a feat worth seeking out.
Theseus mapped every inch of the labyrinth before he struck. Thorough exploration is its own reward — the dungeon does not give its secrets to those who rush.
Hermes moved between worlds faster than thought. Those who master teleportation in the dungeon earn the messenger’s favor and find that distance becomes a matter of will.
The Sibyl spoke ten thousand prophecies from memory. Early mastery of many subjects impresses even the gods — and the dungeon’s quiz system rewards breadth as much as depth.
Ahasverus walked forever and never rested. Those who walk far enough in a single dungeon run see things that only patience and persistence reveal.
Ariadne gave her thread so the hero could escape the maze swiftly when the deed was done. Speed through a level after you have done what you came to do is a form of grace.
Morgan le Fay cast her most powerful spells from the very edge of ruin. Desperation sharpens magic — some spells work better when you have almost nothing left.
Cu Chulainn’s battle-fury was born from fear itself. Some warriors in the dungeon fight best when the situation looks most desperate.
Sisyphus’ boulder rests on a steep slope in the deep dungeon. The Fates respect those who bear burdens willingly and keep climbing regardless.
The Fisher King waits to be healed. A rare herb grows near his resting place, and faith is the medicine that makes the herb work. Those who know both will find him grateful.
Near the sixtieth depth, an ancient wounded king waits in silence. The cure for his condition grows on his own floor — the dungeon provides what the dungeon takes.
Odin hung on the World Tree for nine days and nine nights to gain wisdom. A similar vigil is said to grant extraordinary sight in the dungeon — patience is the price.
The Mithridates Protocol: survive your own poison again and again, in small doses, until it cannot touch you. The dungeon offers many opportunities to practice.
Prometheus burned forever and yet lived. Those who endure bleeding without dying grow stronger for it — the dungeon remembers who kept moving when they should have stopped.

A chainsaw-wielding survivor from the world above has been spotted in the dungeon — loud, brash, and carrying a weapon that cannot be set down. His other hand holds a boomstick, and his satchel contains a book that should not be opened.
A white-haired monster hunter walks the dungeon armed with a silver blade and five learned Signs. He carries three potions on his belt and knows his enemies better than they know themselves.

A young woman with elder blood in her veins begins the dungeon already knowing how to step sideways through space. She carries a blade called Zireael and is faster than almost any creature she will face.

A young trainer in a red-and-white cap has been seen descending into the dungeon, carrying not weapons but soul spheres. Those who have watched him say he does not fight his battles alone — he fights them through what he has captured.
The First Magic Word was said to tear reality apart and send the speaker elsewhere. Some say speaking it still works, if you know where to type it — the dungeon has a memory older than its stones.
Adventurers sometimes tell of useless scraps of leather they find discarded in corridors. Those who held onto them long enough discovered they were not useless at all.
